items.note: The single most useful word in Japan. Use it to get a waiter's attention, to apologise for bumping into someone, or to ask a stranger for help. More versatile than ありがとう — when in doubt, say すみません. Literally it means 'it doesn't end' (the feeling of debt toward you).

items.script: ありがとうございます
items.kana: ありがとうございます
items.note: The formal version. ありがとう alone is casual and fine with friends or shopkeepers. Saying ありがとうございました (past tense) as you leave a shop or restaurant is slightly more elegant — it frames the whole experience as something you are grateful for.

Japanese people often avoid flat いいえ (too blunt) and use ちょっと... (chotto... = 'a little...') or 難しいですね (muzukashii desu ne = 'that's difficult...') to soften a no. A confident はい is often used just to signal you're listening, not necessarily agreeing.

Past tense of わかる (to understand). Saying わかりました signals you've grasped something. わかります (present) means 'I understand (in general)'. わかりません means 'I don't understand' — use it freely, Japanese people won't be offended.
Don't be embarrassed to say this. Follow it with もう一度お願いします (please say it again) or ゆっくり話してください (please speak slowly). Most Japanese people are patient with foreign visitors.

items.script: もう一度お願いします
items.kana: もういちどおねがいします
items.note: もう = again/more, 一度 = once, お願いします = please (literally 'I make a request'). お願いします alone is a very useful polite closer to any request — like adding 'please' at the end.

items.script: ゆっくり話してください
items.kana: ゆっくりはなしてください
items.note: ゆっくり = slowly/leisurely (an adverb). 話して is the て-form of 話す (to speak). ください = please (from the verb くださる, a polite form of くれる, 'to give'). The て-form + ください is the standard way to make a polite request.

items.script: 日本語が少し話せます
items.kana: にほんごがすこしはなせます
english: I can speak a little Japanese
items.note: A great opener. 少し (sukoshi = a little) manages expectations. 話せます is the potential form of 話す (to speak) — adding せ before ます turns it into 'can speak'. Japanese people genuinely appreciate any effort with the language, however small.

Said before every meal, hands sometimes briefly joined. いただく is the humble form of もらう (to receive) or 食べる (to eat). It acknowledges gratitude to everyone involved — the farmer, the cook, the animal. There is no English equivalent; 'bon appétit' is the closest. Not saying it won't cause offence, but saying it will impress.

english: thank you for the feast [said after eating]
items.note: Said when you finish eating, as you leave a restaurant, or to a host. ご馳走 (gochisou) literally means 'running around' — it evokes the effort of gathering ingredients. さま is an honorific. Past tense (でした) covers the whole meal. Cashiers and chefs will smile when they hear it.

What to say at the restaurant entrance when asked 何名様ですか (nanmei-sama desu ka = how many people?). 一人 = one person, 二人 = two people (futari), 三人 = three people (sannin). The reception staff will often just hold up fingers — match them.

Useful when no menu is offered automatically. In Japan, many restaurants display plastic food models (食品サンプル, shokuhin sanpuru) outside — pointing at those and saying これをください (kore wo kudasai = please give me this one) is perfectly acceptable.

The tourist's best friend. これ = this (near me), それ = that (near you), あれ = that (over there). Point at the menu photo, the plastic display, or the next table's dish and say これをください. を is the object marker, ください = please give me.

items.kana: おすすめはなんですか
items.note: おすすめ = recommendation (from 勧める, to recommend). Japanese chefs and waiters are proud of their seasonal specialities — asking this often gets an enthusiastic response and sometimes something off-menu. Nod, say なるほど (naruhodo = I see) and look interested.

うまい (umai) is the rougher, male-coded version. おいしい is universally polite. Exaggerate your expression — a sincere おいしい！ with wide eyes means a lot to a cook. The kanji is 美味しい (beautiful + taste). You can also say 最高です (saikou desu = it's the best!).

items.script: お会計をお願いします
items.kana: おかいけいをおねがいします
items.note: 会計 = accounting/bill, お is an honorific prefix. In Japan you almost never catch a waiter's eye and mime signing — you say this or go to the register. Many restaurants have a rule that you pay at the exit (お会計はレジで, okaikei wa reji de). Tip: bills are not split by default; ask 別々でお願いします (betsubetsu de onegaishimasu) for separate bills.

items.kana: べつべつでおねがいします
items.note: 別 (betsu) means 'separate/different'. Doubling it (別々) intensifies: 'each separate'. で is the means particle (by means of / in the manner of). This is unusual to ask in Japan — going dutch isn't the cultural norm — but it's perfectly understood and accepted.

The most important direction phrase. Replace ～ with what you're looking for: 駅はどこですか (eki wa doko desu ka = where is the station?), トイレはどこですか (toire wa doko desu ka = where is the toilet?). どこ = where, は is the topic marker.

まっすぐ行って (massugu itte) = go straight. Key direction words: 右 (migi = right), 左 (hidari = left), 角 (kado = corner), 信号 (shingou = traffic light), 交差点 (kousaten = intersection). Japanese directions often use landmarks rather than street names.

右に曲がってください (migi ni magatte kudasai) = please turn right. 左に曲がってください = please turn left. 曲がる (magaru) = to turn. に is the direction particle. Japanese people giving directions will often physically turn and gesture — watch their body, not just the words.

近い (chikai) = near/close. The opposite is 遠い (tooi) = far. 歩いていけますか (aruite ikemasu ka) = can I walk there? 何分ですか (nampun desu ka) = how many minutes? Japan is extremely walkable in city centres — 10 minutes on foot is the usual threshold.

items.script: この地図を見せてもらえますか
items.kana: このちずをみせてもらえますか
romaji: kono chizu wo misete moraemasu ka
english: could you show me on this map?
items.note: Show them your phone's map app. misete = て-form of 見せる (to show), もらえますか = 'could I receive?' (can you do this for me?). This is much more effective than trying to understand spoken directions — Japanese people are very willing to point on a map.

迷子 literally means 'lost child' and is used by adults too without embarrassment. になりました = became. Say this to a police officer (交番 kouban = police box, found everywhere), a shop clerk, or a station attendant. Showing your hotel's business card (名刺 meishi) or the address on your phone is the clearest solution.

いくら = how much (money). いくら is also the Japanese word for salmon roe — don't let that confuse you. Prices are almost always displayed in Japan, so you'll often know before asking. Consumption tax (消費税 shouhizei, 10%) is included in displayed prices since 2021.

items.script: クレジットカードは使えますか
items.kana: くれじっとかーどはつかえますか
english: can I use a credit card?
items.note: Japan is still largely a cash society, especially outside big cities and tourist spots. Convenience stores (コンビニ konbini), large chains, and department stores accept cards. Small ramen shops, shrines, local restaurants — often cash only. Keep 5,000–10,000 yen in cash on hand. IC cards like Suica (スイカ) work on transport and many shops.

items.script: 試着してもいいですか
items.kana: しちゃくしてもいいですか
romaji: shichaku shite mo ii desu ka
items.note: 試着 (shichaku) = fitting/trying on clothes. してもいいですか is the standard 'may I?' construction (て-form + も + いい + ですか). The staff will often immediately offer the fitting room when you pick something up — you may not even need to ask.

予約 (yoyaku) = reservation/booking. があります = 'exists' / 'I have'. If they ask your name: 名前はニコラです (namae wa Nikora desu). Have your booking confirmation on your phone — staff will often just look at it.

items.script: チェックアウトをお願いします
items.kana: ちぇっくあうとをおねがいします
items.note: Check-out time in Japan is typically 11:00 or 12:00 (noon). The hotel may offer to store your luggage until later (荷物を預かってもらえますか, nimotsu wo azukatte moraemasu ka = can you store my luggage?). This is almost always free.

items.script: Wi-Fiのパスワードは何ですか
items.kana: わいふぁいのぱすわーどはなんですか
romaji: wai-fai no pasuwaado wa nan desu ka
items.note: Wi-Fi is pronounced ワイファイ (wai-fai) in Japanese. Most hotels, cafés, and convenience stores have free Wi-Fi. Many post the password on a sign near the counter — look before asking. Pocket Wi-Fi rental at the airport is a good option for unlimited data.

まで = until/to. 一枚 = one flat object (tickets, sheets). On local trains, find the fare chart above the ticket machine, note the price, then select that amount. IC cards (Suica, ICOCA) are far easier — tap on, tap off, fare is automatically deducted.

items.kana: しゅうでんはなんじですか
english: what time is the last train?
items.note: 終電 (shuden) = last train (終 = end, 電 = electricity/train). Missing the last train in Japan is a real concern — it usually runs between midnight and 1am depending on the line. The alternative is a taxi (expensive) or a manga café (漫画喫茶 manga kissa) which offers capsule-style overnight stays.

items.script: この電車は～に止まりますか
items.kana: このでんしゃは～にとまりますか
romaji: kono densha wa ~ ni tomarimasu ka
items.note: 止まります (tomarimasu) = stops, from 止まる (tomaru). に is the location/target particle. Japan has express (急行 kyuukou), rapid (快速 kaisoku), and local (各停 kaku-tei) trains on the same line — not every train stops at every station. Check the destination board above the doors.

助ける (tasukeru) = to save/help. The て-form + ください = please do. For medical emergencies, call 119 (ambulance and fire). For police, call 110. Japan has police boxes (交番 kouban) on most busy streets — they are extremely helpful, even for directions and lost items.

english: I feel unwell / I feel sick
items.note: 気分 (kibun) = feeling/mood, 悪い = bad. 熱があります (netsu ga arimasu) = I have a fever. 頭が痛いです (atama ga itai desu) = I have a headache. 薬局 (yakkyoku) = pharmacy, found everywhere. Many pharmacies stock basic English-labelled medicines.

items.kana: おとしものをしました
english: I lost something / I dropped something
items.note: 落とし物 (otoshimono) = lost item. Japan has an exceptional lost-and-found culture — wallets, phones, and bags are routinely handed in to police boxes. Go to the nearest 交番 (kouban). The national lost property database is centralised. Items found on trains go to the station's service desk (駅員室 ekiin-shitsu).
